narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 43% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, family background, and planned study schedule, focusing on patterns seen in thousands of past students without considering gender or race. The most influential factor pushing toward this higher failure risk was your plan to attend part-time, as the vast majority of students who passed—over 93%—went full-time, allowing more focused preparation that strongly correlates with bar success. On a more positive note, your third-year grade decile of 7 outperformed the average of about 5.9 among those who passed, which helped pull the prediction toward success by showing your ability to rank well later in school. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 exceeded the 3.24 average for bar passers, signaling solid foundational strength and countering some of the risk. While your LSAT score of 40 and upper-middle family income aligned well with successful applicants—your income bracket matches nearly half of those who passed—they played supporting roles amid these top drivers. Overall, your profile stacks up competitively in grades and scores against admitted students, but the part-time commitment stood out as the biggest deviation from their typical full-time paths. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background; it's the model highlighting where risks align with past outcomes. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time plan, as that single change could dramatically shift your predicted odds. You might also strengthen your case by retaking the LSAT for an even higher score or gathering letters emphasizing your later academic gains. Many students in similar positions succeed by addressing these key areas—reach out to admissions for personalized advice on building a stronger profile next time. With your strong deciles and GPA as a foundation, you're already ahead in ways that matter.
